danger_signals.visual_form: Low score indicators, failed attempt feedback
danger_signals.meaning: Player is not executing the athletic event correctly or with sufficient quality

danger_signals.visual_form: Competing athlete progresses further or scores higher in head-to-head events
danger_signals.meaning: Player is losing the competition and risk elimination from tournament

safety_signals.visual_form: High score display, athlete crosses finish line or completes action
safety_signals.meaning: Player has performed the athletic event successfully and advances

safety_signals.visual_form: Selected country flag displayed during Olympic mode
safety_signals.meaning: Player is established in safe, legitimate competitive framework with national identity
Safe practice environment without competitive consequence

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: National flags (Argentina, Australia, Belgium, Brazil, Canada, China, France, West Germany, Great Britain, Japan, Kenya, South Korea, Poland, Soviet Union, United States, etc.)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Competing athletes from other nations appear as opponents in versus and Olympic modes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Country selection screen, athlete portraits associated with national representation, flag display during competition

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Olympic rings/Championship branding (varied by regional version)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Menu screens, password entry screens, mode selection between Training/Olympic/Versus/Championship

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Japanese domestic release (Konami Sports in Seoul)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Full graphical detail on password screen with athlete graphic, red border, decorative bow, underlines; cigarette and ashtray visible on typewriter screen; detailed character portraits; staff roll credits included; Olympic torch with specific shading; gray-out non-selected menu items

Simplified password screen (centered, no athlete graphic, no red border, no bow, blue text); cigarette and ashtray removed from typewriter screen (Nintendo of America policy compliance); redrawn character portraits; staff roll credits omitted; torch icon different shading; different background colors during gameplay; no gray-out on non-selected items
Menu centered in Revision 2; 'Olympic' changed to 'Championship' in Revision 2; password symbols adjusted between versions
Country representation system active; player selects national identity; multi-round tournament structure with progression
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player competes across multiple events representing their chosen nation against world competitors

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Two-player head-to-head competition interface; direct opposition framing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Real-time competitive play between two human players in individual events

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: NES Zapper optional controller support enabled; shooting-based event visual presentation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Light-gun shooting mechanics replace standard controller input for this specific event

environmental_storytelling.example: Removal of cigarettes/ashtray in Western versions signals cultural/regulatory differences in target market; Olympic branding changed to Championship shows geopolitical context shift

environmental_storytelling.technique: National flag system as faction/allegiance display
environmental_storytelling.example: 28 selectable countries (including Soviet Union, Cold War era geopolitics) make player's national affiliation explicit and central to Olympic mode narrative

environmental_storytelling.technique: Sporting event selection as world capability display
environmental_storytelling.example: 15 diverse events (fencing, taekwondo, shooting, swimming, gymnastics, etc.) represent international athletic traditions and national strengths

Character portraits deliberately redrawn between Japanese and Western versions; Japanese versions noted as appearing 'melting' suggests visual quality/technical constraints communicated through character appearance
Training/Olympic/Versus/Exhibition modes structure narrative progression from safe practice to competitive representation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Practices individual events without competitive pressure; isolated from opponent AI
Represents chosen nation; progresses through tournament rounds; faces algorithmically-scaling AI opponents from other countries
Competes directly against second human player in head-to-head event format
Performs mirrored or complementary athletic action in real-time; visually progresses or regresses based on input timing and execution
Non-selected options display as grayed-out graphic versions; full visual detail on password interface; credits accessible
Non-selected options invisible or unmarked; simplified password interface; censored elements (cigarettes); no credits accessible

notes: Track & Field II uses national representation as primary faction system rather than explicit antagonistic forces. World-state communication is primarily achieved through regional visual censorship/modification (cigarettes, artwork style) and mode-based context switching (Training/Olympic/Versus). The game's manifestation layer relies on flag iconography, athlete visual state progression, and menu structure to communicate competitive context and player agency. No explicit danger/safety signals exist beyond event success/failure feedback.
